questions.prompt: Why is schema-per-tenant rough at 10,000+ tenants?

choices.text: Operational complexity scales poorly. Schema migrations must run on each schema (10K migrations to add a column); even with parallelization, this takes hours and risks failures mid-migration. Postgres catalog grows with schemas; some tooling slows or breaks at thousands of schemas. Connection pool management gets hairy (which schema is each connection set to? Re-binding per request adds latency). Schema introspection in dev tools and migrations slows down. Listing/iterating tenants requires querying pg_catalog. For 10s to low 1000s of tenants, schema-per-tenant works well; beyond that, pooled-with-RLS (single shared schema, isolation enforced by database-level policies) scales much better. The architecture decision is a function of tenant count: 10-100 tenants → schema-per-tenant viable; 1000s+ → pooled+RLS unless you really need per-tenant DBs. Counterintuitively, the highly-isolated 'silo' (DB-per-tenant) can be more operationally manageable than schema-per-tenant at scale because each DB is independent and tooling treats them as separate entities.

questions.explanation: Migrations × N schemas + tooling slowdowns + connection complexity. Scales poorly past 1000s.

questions.prompt: EU customer demands their data stay in EU. Your SaaS is US-only. How?

choices.text: Add an EU deployment (DB + app servers in EU region); route EU customers to it via the tenants table — `tenant.region = 'eu'` determines which DB / app cluster handles their requests. Auth middleware looks up tenant; routes accordingly. Patterns: full regional split (US tenants stay US-only; EU tenants stay EU-only), or active-active replication with conflict resolution (complex, rare). For most SaaS: regional split — one binary, configured per region, customer data lives in its declared region. Tools like CockroachDB, Spanner, YugabyteDB handle this natively for SQL workloads; for Postgres, you provision separate clusters per region and route. Compliance: data residency is a real legal requirement for GDPR-sensitive customers; can't just lie. Implementation cost: significant first time (auth routing, deployment automation, region-aware operations); minimal incremental cost per additional region. Charge enterprise tier for data residency — it's a real cost; revenue should reflect. Most SaaS add regions in this order: US (default) → EU → APAC → others by demand.

questions.explanation: Add EU deployment; route EU tenants to EU DB via tenants table. Charge enterprise tier for the cost.
